summary: On page reload the ?preview=<id> URL param takes precedence over the sessionStorage draft, silently discarding unsaved edits to the previewed route.
failure_scenario: Open /routeBuilder?preview=42, make edits (draft saved with editingRouteId=42), press F5: #maybeRestorePendingRoute hits the preview branch before the #isPageReload draft branch, #loadRouteForEditing(42) runs #emptyRoute (whose #saveDraft with 0 waypoints deletes the draft) and refetches the saved version — all unsaved edits lost with no confirm.

summary: #submit has no in-flight guard and never disables the confirm button, so double-click or auto-repeating Enter fires multiple POST /saveRoute calls that each create a route.
failure_scenario: User double-clicks Save (or holds Enter in #route-name-input): two+ POSTs sent before the first response hides the modal → duplicate routes created server-side, #onSaved runs per response so editingRouteId ends on whichever lands last, and the saved-routes list shows N copies.

summary: The one-neighborhood check uses queryRenderedFeatures via #regionIdAtPoint, which returns null for any point outside the current viewport, so an off-screen geocoded address bypasses the different-region refusal and is silently snapped to the nearest street of the locked region.
failure_scenario: Start point in region R, user types an End address in a different neighborhood: geocoded point projects off-canvas → pointRegionId null → no warning; snapToStreet(lngLat, R) picks the R street nearest the faraway address, extending the route to an arbitrary boundary point nowhere near the typed address, no feedback.

summary: #loadRouteForEditing has no fetch sequencing/abort guard, so with two loads in flight the last response — not the last click — wins.
failure_scenario: Slow network, click saved-route card A then card B: B's fetch resolves first (B drawn, editingRouteId=B), then A's stale response arrives, passes the streetsRendered check, calls #emptyRoute (wiping B) and draws A — user clicked B last but is silently editing A.

summary: Reconstructing a loaded saved route by A*-routing between per-street boundary waypoints substitutes the shorter of two parallel edges sharing both endpoint nodes; street count unchanged so no 'route-adjusted' toast fires and the save button shows 'Saved'.
failure_scenario: A saved route traverses the longer of two edges connecting the same intersections (crescent vs straight block); on load, route() relaxes to the shorter edge → map shows and a subsequent Update persists a different street than the user saved, with no toast.

summary: #reconstructPath decides traversal direction by testing whether the entry node is within 15 m of the street's first coordinate, mis-assigning flip for streets 10-15 m long whose both endpoints are within that radius.
failure_scenario: Route passes through a short connector street (10-15 m) entered at its last coordinate: distance(entryNode, coords[0]) still < 15 m → flip=false instead of true → direction arrows reversed and wrong reverse flag saved in /saveRoute payload, so Explore walks that street backwards.

summary: Reverse-geocode responses have no ordering guard, so an earlier, slower response can overwrite the field label and #endpointStreets for a newer endpoint position.
failure_scenario: User extends the route twice quickly (end point moves >15 m each time): first reverse-geocode resolves after the second → End field and #endpointStreets.end show the previous endpoint's street, and #suggestedRouteName proposes a stale 'X to Y' name in the save modal.

summary: When a loaded route's street ids no longer exist in the loaded street data, #loadRouteForEditing returns silently after #emptyRoute already wiped the current route — blank map, no error, no editing session.
failure_scenario: Click a saved-route card whose streets were all removed by filterLowQuality (or ids no longer match): #unsavedWorkConfirmed discards the in-progress route, #emptyRoute clears the map, #drawStreetList returns [] → early return with no toast — current route gone, nothing loads.
